questions.question: Explain the concept of a one-dimensional array with an example. How is memory allocated for a one-dimensional array?
keyPoints: Array is a collection of elements of the same data type
Stored in contiguous memory locations
Example: int marks[5] stores 5 integers
Memory address of each element = base + (index × element_size)
All elements accessed using index starting from 0

questions.question: What are the advantages and limitations of using arrays in C programming?
keyPoints: Advantages: Stores multiple values under one name, efficient random access O(1), easy iteration with loops, contiguous memory allocation
Limitations: Fixed size (must know at compile time), no bounds checking, wastage of memory if not fully used, difficult insertion/deletion

questions.question: Explain the difference between row-major and column-major storage of two-dimensional arrays with an example.
keyPoints: Row-major: Elements stored row by row (C language default)
Column-major: Elements stored column by column (Fortran default)
Example: For 2×2 array {{a,b},{c,d}}, row-major stores: a,b,c,d
Column-major stores: a,c,b,d
Memory address calculation differs based on order

questions.question: Write a C program to find the sum and average of n elements stored in an array.
keyPoints: Declare array and variables for sum, average, n
Input n and array elements using loop
Calculate sum by adding all elements
Calculate average = sum/n
Display results with appropriate formatting

questions.question: Explain the relationship between array name and pointer in C with an example.
keyPoints: Array name without brackets acts as pointer to first element
arr == &arr[0] both give address of first element
arr[i] is equivalent to *(arr+i)
When array passed to function, passed by reference (pointer)
Pointer arithmetic works differently for arrays

questions.question: How do you initialize a two-dimensional array? Explain with code examples.
keyPoints: Complete initialization: int arr[2][3] = {{1,2,3},{4,5,6}};
Partial initialization: int arr[2][3] = {1,2,3,4}; fills row-wise
Initialize all to zero: int arr[2][3] = {0};
Without size: int arr[][3] = {{1,2,3},{4,5,6}}; (only first dimension can be omitted)

questions.question: Write a C program to find the largest and smallest element in an array.
keyPoints: Initialize max and min with first element (arr[0])
Traverse array from index 1 to n-1
Compare each element with max/min and update accordingly
Use single loop for efficiency
Print final max and min values

questions.question: Write a C program to perform matrix addition of two 3×3 matrices. Explain the logic and memory representation.
keyPoints: Declare two 3×3 matrices and result matrix
Accept elements for both matrices using nested loops
For addition: result[i][j] = mat1[i][j] + mat2[i][j]
Use two nested loops (i for rows, j for columns)
Display all three matrices in proper format
Row-major storage: elements of each row stored consecutively
Total 9 elements per matrix in contiguous memory

questions.question: Explain multidimensional arrays in C. Declare a three-dimensional array and explain how elements are stored and accessed in memory.
keyPoints: Multidimensional arrays extend 1D and 2D concepts
Declaration: int arr[2][3][4] - 2 planes, 3 rows, 4 columns
Total elements = 2×3×4 = 24
Stored in row-major order within each dimension
Access: arr[plane][row][col]
Memory address = base + ((p×R + r) × C + c) × size
Example: arr[1][2][3] accessed using nested loops

questions.question: Write a C program to search an element in an array using linear search and binary search. Compare both algorithms.
keyPoints: Linear Search: Check each element sequentially until found or end reached, O(n)
Binary Search: Requires sorted array, divide search space in half each time, O(log n)
Implement both with proper input handling
Binary search: compare with middle element, eliminate half
Show time complexity comparison
Discuss when to use each method

questions.question: Explain the concept of passing arrays to functions in C. Write a function to sort an array using bubble sort and explain the concept of call by reference.
keyPoints: Array name passed acts as pointer to first element
Function receives base address, not a copy of array
Changes made in function affect original array
Pass array name and size as parameters
Bubble Sort: Repeatedly swap adjacent elements if in wrong order
Optimize with flag to detect if already sorted
Time complexity O(n²), space O(1)
Explain why array is not duplicated (efficiency)
